content.content: A new year begins. The economic crisis is worsening throughout the world, with no end in sight. All very worrisome trends.
Unemployment continues to spiral upwards.
A works program has been enacted in order to reduce unemployment.
However, unemployment remains too high for comfort.
There is little to no good news in Saskatchewan. The economic depression has still not bottomed out.
We have begun to launch a public works program, but unemployment is still stubbornly high. The people are beginning to finally have optimism, however.
We have begun to launch a public works program, and unemployment is finally starting to fall. The people are beginning to finally have optimism.
The economic depression seems to have bottomed out, but there is still much suffering. It is not too late to launch an economic rescue program.
We have begun to launch a public works program, but the economy is still troubled. The recovery has begun, but more must be done.
Our public works program has successfully guided us through the worst of the crisis. The economy is improving, and finally people have optimism for the future.
With the return to normalcy, the political landscape in Saskatchewan is shifting. Liberalism is making a return among the middle classes, and the radical parties of the right are declining.
The prime minister is in poor health; he is not expected to live out the year.

content.content: Requires 1 budget.

With our policy of land reform, we are acquiring farmland from large estates, and giving the land to farmworkers' associations and small peasants. In essence, this brings class struggle to the countryside, breaking the semi-feudal property relations present in East City Police and other areas with large estates. The bourgeois parties and large farmers despise our intrusion into the sacredness of private property, while leftists are cautiously optimistic.

The police have historically been overrun with reactionaries, and we are trying to change this, little by little. We have improved the training and recruitment process to select for a broader demographic than the traditional conservatives, and to put a tighter leash on their political activity. In addition, we set up committees to investigate the ranks of the police and remove those with far-right sympathies. It is a slow and arduous work.

A New Day in Saskatchewan

After the death of Richard Bennett and the Federal election of 1934, it seems as if an era of history is closing. The worst years of political and economic crisis are over, and SCPSsm's days are numbered. Perhaps Saskatchewan will finally enter into a new era of genuine peace...

content.content: A young upstart

content: For decades, Saskatchewan has been ruled by the Liberal and Conservative parties. Under these two, the province has developed into a promising new western province. Saskatchewan has become a chief producer of the modern gold,

content.content: wheat

content: . Immigrants across the globe have been attracted to its vasst swathes of unclaimed farmland waiting to be tilled.

content.content: Yet under these two hegemons, Saskatchewan has been taken as a cow to be milked. Under the Liberals, the provincial government had been hesitant towards farmer's demands for protections against large grain and transportation companies, forcing to the farmers to construct their own independent cooperatives. The Conservatives and Progressives' mismanagement of Saskatchewan's treasury has left the province in deep debt with barely anything to show for it.

The time is ripe for a new force of change in the province. The FLP has been formed as a merger of the ILP, an urban labour party, and the UFC(SS) backed UFPA, an agrarian socialist organization. Therefore, we strive to represent all the people struck low in these dark times.

Terrorists Struggle to Feed Families

Since the mid-1920s, the Ku Klux Klan has found a home in farmer Saskatchewan. The KKK has turned many Anglo-Protestant settlers against the new immigrant farmers coming in from Eastern Europe, many of them Orthodox Christian, with rhetoric calling for a pure, British society. This comes in addition to lighting up existing tensions with the French settlers and Catholics. Thus, KKK membership had reportedly peaked at 47,000, around 1 in 10 Saskatchewanians.

However, with both heightening drought conditions and the Great Depression underway, membership has been greatly diminished while reports of KKK terrorism have gradually been declining. This is largely attributed to it's large base tending to their families in order to survive these difficult times.

This will almost surely have great effect on Saskatchewan politics. Anderson's Cooperative Coalition had maintained unofficial ties with the KKK throughout the late 1920s and some of those in high-ranking government positions are openly members of the organization. With the KKK now reduced, Anderson will have to find a new ally to survive the upcoming 1934 General Election

content.content: The Progressives fall out of relevance

The Progressive Party of Saskatchewan, once a key coalition partner of the Anderson government, has formally disbanded. Initially a leading voice of rural discontent, the party's appeal has been greatly diminished by the decision of the UFC(SS) to form its own political party. The Progressive MLAs have been left astray.

MLA Jacob Benson has chosen to join our party and sit as a Farmer-Labour representative and has chosen to continue supporting the Cooperative Coalition. Through him, we can finally exercise our weight in government. MLA Charles Agar has chosen to sit with the Grits. The remaining 3 MLAs have either joined the Conservative party or have retired from politics.
With the Progressives now out the picture, rural Saskatchewan seems ripe for the taking by our party.

game_over.needham_wins.title: Needham is in undisputed control over Saskatchewan.

content.content: Needham is in undisputed control over Saskatchewan. The FLP, along with all of the democratic forces, have failed.

The Province of Saskatchewan has a unicameral legislature, the Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan. The Premier is the head of government, and is the leader of the party who has the confidence of a majority of the house. The Premier appoints a cabinet of ministers from elected MLAs to oversee various departments of government.

The Legislative Assembly is primarily elected based on a first-past-the-post system divided into various electoral districts, colloquially referred to as "ridings". Some ridings are multi-member, elected using block voting. The candidate elected in each riding becomes an MLA (Member of the Legislative Assembly).
The Province of Saskatchewan oversees various policy areas, including education, healthcare, gambling, and agriculture, among others.

Local politics are traditionally nonpartisan but can still be ideological. There are two main types of local government: Urban (UM) and Rural Municipalities (RM). Rural municipalities are traditionally more dominant in our sparsely populated province and have cooperated to form the Sasakatchewan Association of Rural Municipalities (SARM).

content.content: The more radical workers despise our suppression of their strikes, recalling the days of Friedrich Ebert.

While a 40-hour work week was declared during the Saskatchewanian Revolution in 1918, enforcement has become increasingly porous over the years. The capitalists have successfully added numerous exceptions and loopholes, returning to the old regime of forced overtime for workers.
Now, with the force of the Labour Ministry, we are finally enforcing the rights of workers to their own time. The bourgeoisie and their political representatives are displeased.
We have increased welfare spending. Our proletarian base is pleased.

content.content: Selection of our President

The time has come for the FLP to select a President. Five distinguished party members have been nominated though 3 have already declined to run. That leaves 2, George Williams and Major James Coldwell.

George Williams is the architect behind the Saskatchewan farmer's entrance into politics. Having served as President of the UFC(SS), he steered the organization against endorsing the disorganized and ineffective PPS, instead attempting to create his own Farmer's party. He is a radical agrarian socialist and does not back away from criticizing Capitalism for the plight of the workers and farmers.

Major James Coldwell is the moderate former leader of the ILP and founder of the urban wing of the PPS. As a school principal, he championed teacher's labour rights. Coldwell is a strong public speaker and would be able to draw large crowds in the cities. He holds a grudge against Williams for not endorsing the PPS, contributing to an embarrassing election defeat.
Coldwell will lead us to victory!

As the UFC (SS) is no longer officially affiliated with us, we will have a harder time recruiting farmers to our cause.

Attacking the far right is popular among the entire party, although the left considers the bourgeois parties to be just as dangerous, while the right is critical of our lack of response to the CP(S). Our bourgeois coalition partners are also skeptical of our lack of commitment to anti-communism.

media.commercial.title: Make our newspapers more commercialized by broadening their appeal beyond party supporters.
media.commercial.subtitle: This might upset ideological purists, but it may bring in more funds and perhaps expose the middle class to socialist views.

content.content: Our commercialized newspapers are able to bring in more funds. However, the left and labour factions express their displeasure.

In an emergency conference, we have decided to become a people's party that encompasses all of the regular people of Saskatchewan, not just the workers and urban poor. The Left and Agrarians claim that our new party platform is a betrayal of our founding principles, a turn away from Marxism towards bourgeois opportunism. Whatever the case, we are now a party representing all of the people, not just the proletariat.
Should we run a new campaign to publicize our new platform?
We have promised to adopt a more moderate posture. This may alienate the left, however.
We do not need these new ideas; they seem to imitate too closely those of the SCPSs and Communists.
Neorevisionism: A New Idea

Several young socialists, led by Carlo Mierendorff, are calling for a "new revisionism" in response to the SCPSs threat. This political tendency calls for a mass movement in favor of democracy in order to counter the SCPSs and Communist movements, and to update our rhetoric and imagery to appeal to emotion and not just reason.
As we have already funded resources towards the UFC (SS), we can invest in building up their militancy.
We develop an entire campaign against antisemitism under the auspices of the Iron Front. Hopefully our campaign can sway the workers away from SCPSsm.
Enacting this policy would allow farmers to remain on their land by leasing the property back to their creditor or a public authority.
This would enable the farmer to utilize their land to pay back their payments, acting as a safeguard from the seizure of their primary form of income generation.
We would construct a Farm Land Security Board to oversee this policy and ensure that both farmers and creditors are acting faithfully.
This is a radical farmer policy that would potentially destabilize our agricultural sector. However, it may be worth it to ensure the support of the farmers.
We have increased training and purchased more armaments for the UFC(SS).
The PPS Party decries the militarization of the UFC(SS), and its members are leaving the republican organization.
As a people's party, we represent the employees and small businesspeople of the middle class, and they are becoming receptive to our messages.

options.title: Yes, we will promise this policy.

Given our defeat in the Prussian coup, the far-right appears to be ascendant.
However, Prime Minister Brüning's austerity policies and the continuation of the depression are paving the way for Needham, or someone like him, to take power.
Timeline of Saskatchewan

content: : Henry Kelsey explores the land that would come to be known as Saskatchewan

: The establishment of Manitoba as a province initiates a wave of Métis migration into the South Saskatchewan River Basin. Saskatchewan becomes the hotbed of Métis and Indigenous resistance against the encroachment of Canada.
: The Dominion Lands Act is passed, allowing individuals to claim up to 160 acres of Prairie land for free. Settlers gradually begin to pour into the province.

: The Provisional Government of Saskatchewan is declared by a group of Métis along the Southbranch settlements. They are joined by a few hundred Cree and Assiniboine. Thus, the Northwest-Rebellion is declared. The rebellion is put down a month later at the Battle of Batoche by Canadian militia and the NWMP.

: The Canadian Pacific Railway is completed, granting complete access to the Prairies and commencing another wave of immigration, primarily to service the agricultural sector. Many immigrants come from Germany, The United Kingdom, and Ukraine.
: Saskatchewan is inaugurated as a province. In the resulting election, the Liberal Party comes to power with the support of the farmers.

: The Progressive Party of Canada is formed primarily to represent the small family farms. They win the second most seats in the 1921 Canadian Federal Election though cede the title of "Official Opposition" to the CPC due to party infighting.
: 46,000 farmers sign up to form the Saskatchewan Wheat Pool, an agricultural cooperative.

: In the 1929 general election, the Conservatives, Progressives, and a number of Independents form a coalition government after the Liberals secure only a plurality of seats. James Anderson becomes Premier and head of the "Cooperative Coalition".
: The Great Depression hits Saskatchewan, compounded by the Dust Bowl and the province's reliance on commodity sale. Thousands emigrate from the family farm. Per capita money income falls 75%.

: Following an intense campaign by farmers, the Cooperative Coalition attempts to implement a 100% grain marketing board, but is blocked by the courts. A gas tax is introduced to make up for falling government revenues. Meanwhile, both the Coalition and Liberals vote down a motion to cut public sector salaries.

: Striking workers at the Bienfait Coal Mine collaborate with the WUL to demand better working conditions from the American owners. When scabs are prevented from entering the mine, the RCMP is brought in, resulting in a riot and the deaths of 3 mine workers.
: The Cooperative Coalition now resorts to retrenchment, observing the shattering rise in the provincial deficit.
: The FLP is formed by a merger of the ILP and the UFC(SS)'s political apparatus.

content.content: "Emergency measures, however, are of only temporary value, for the present depression is a sign of the mortal sickness of the whole capitalist system, and this sickness cannot be cured by the application of salves. These leave untouched the cancer which is eating at the heart of our society, namely, the economic system in which our natural resources and our principal means of production and distribution are owned, controlled and operated for the private profit of a small proportion of our population.

No CCF Government will rest content until it has eradicated capitalism and put into operation the full programme of socialized planning which will lead to the establishment in Canada of the Cooperative Commonwealth."
- Regina Manifesto, 1933

content.content: This game is a total conversion mod of Autumn Chen's great game - Social Democracy: An Alternate History. It is based on the Saskatchewan CCF/NDP, a social democratic party that governed the Canadian province of Saskatchewan for much of the 20th century.

For more information on the politics and government of Saskatchewan, see the "Library" page on the top left. If this is your first time playing the game, it is strongly recommended that you read at least some of this information.

options.title: Leave the judiciary as it is.

content.content: Judiciary Reform

The Judiciary is one of the most reactionary components of the government, along with the military. Many jurists have held office since the days of the Emperor. They give violent fascists like Needham laughably light sentences, while ruthlessly persecuting leftists and pacifists for lesser crimes.
There is great demand in our membership for reforming the judiciary, although this may bring us into a collision course with our coalition allies.
We have started on the path to judicial reform. While some of the most antideluvian and biased justices have been replaced, many still remain. More should be done.
We have successfully reformed the judiciary, to some extent. At the very least, the courts will not actively obstruct our attempts to prosecute crimes committed by the far-right.

content.content: Labour Affairs

The industrial Ruhr region is aflame with labour unrest. The employers have started a major lockout to force the workers to take more cuts to wages and benefits. There is a need for the Labour Ministry to arbitrate in conflicts between employers and workers.

The argument also goes that this would be complementary to deflation, maintaining the same purchasing power for workers by cutting hours without cutting wages. Employers, however, resist this policy, arguing for more flexibility in hiring and firing.

content.content: Confronting Antisemitism

Antisemitism has long been a latent feature of Saskatchewanian society, but since the beginning of the economic depression, it has grown to a horrifying extent. The SCPSs are the worst of it, but all the right-wing parties are deeply antisemitic. Regarding the FLP specifically, our right-wing enemies love to deploy their racist rhetoric against our many Jewish comrades, magnifying criticism of any minor transgression, and they call the entire FLP a Jewish party. We are a favorite target of their invectives.

We have chosen to add a use-lease policy to our platform. The farmers will surely be enthusiastic.

content.content: The Trek reaches Regina
The On-to-Ottawa trek has finally touched ground in Regina. Prime Minister Bennett has ordered CPR to refuse the trekkers when they attempt to board the trains headed to Winnipeg.

content: As a result, the ~2000 striking workers have been left stranded in the city. Eight trekkers have been dispatched to Ottawa for negotiations. In the meantime,
is begrudgingly supplying the trekkers with food and water.

content.content: This situation has left the FLP leadership divided. Some radical party members hope to rally the FLP in support of the strike, or at least, voice approval. However, many moderates advise caution in affiliating so closely with an event organized by the CP(S)'s WUL.

There are also concerns that Bennett's government will violently suppress the strike. 2 people were killed when Borden's conservative Federal government violently suppressed the 1919 Winnipeg General Strike. In the Bienfait Coal Strike, 3 miners were killed under James Anderson's provincial government.
What must we do?
Fine. We enacted a massive austerity program to cut welfare, in order to maintain the stability of our coalition. The vote has been called off.

content.content: It is a dire time for Saskatchewan. The Great Depression has begun, and the government of James Anderson has resorted to a policy of retrenchment. The government had previously attempted relief with deficit financing but this effort has been halted by business interests. As a result, Anderson's Cooperative Coalition Government has raised taxes on corporations but also levied taxes on gross revenue and oil storage tanks. Civil Service pay has fallen by 9-14%, loans to farmers have all but halted, and the Highways Budget provides for no construction. The province is in the midst of a severe drought, and many farmers are on the brink of ruin.

The Farmer-Labour Group (FLP) is the only party offering a real alternative to the old capitalist order. However, we are a young and inexperienced party, and we face opposition not only from opposing parties but also from within our own ranks, torn between the Agrarian vision of George Williams and the Labour oriented M. J. Coldwell. The FLP's success in the upcoming election will depend on your ability to navigate these challenges and present a compelling vision for the future of Saskatchewan.

content: (if the election were to be held now):

content.content: Detailed results for each demographic

Saskatchewan seems to be one of the hardest to understand provinces in Canada. In the last one hundred years, it has alternated between the staunchly conservative right-wing economics of the Conservative, Liberal, and Saskatchewan Party. Most dominant however, was the CCF/NDP, a Social Democratic Party in a sea of rural voters. It elected the government of Tommy Douglas who Canadians know is famous for introducing Universal Healthcare. It elected Allan Blakeney who directed government ownership in the province's prestigious potash reserves. Yet it also elected the government of Grant Devine, a follower of Reaganomics who sent much of the provincial welfare state and crown corporations into deep depths underground.
This picture becomes clearer when we paint the colourful picture of Saskatchewan history.

At it's inauguration as a province in 1905, Saskatchewan was dominated by the agricultural sector. Everything revovled around wheat, from the land, the people, and what little industry made home in the province. Farmer's organizations such as the SGGA, the FUC, and later the UFC(SS) becmae important lobbying groups. Accordingly, the provincial Liberals, who were proponents of Free Trade and popular among the immigrant farmers, were the dominant political force up until the Great Depression. The provincial Liberals presided over a golden age of Saskatchewan growth, never to be repeated in the next century.

The only break in Liberal dominance was the 1929 election, wherein a coalition of the CPS, PPS, and independents ousted the Liberal Party in a general opposition. Partially attributed to this oddity was the rise of the KKK in Saskatchewan, which supported Conservative leader JTM Anderson's nativist campaign. However, celebrations among the Cooperative Coalition were soon to be extinguished.

The Great Depression hit Saskatchewan the hardest of all the provinces, especially the farmers who observed a 90% decrease in income per capita. Thousands filed into the cities looking for work only to be turned away by what little non-agricultural industry and retail existed.

This struggle brewed a strong localized form of socialism, more impacted by British Fabianism and the Social Gospel than Marxism. From this brew sprouted the FLP, a merger of the UFPA, the political arm of the UFC(SS), the dominant provincial farmer's organization, and the ILP, a miniscule urban socialist party whose leadership was dominated by middle class professionals rather than trade unionists. Accordingly, the early FLP was dominated by its agrarian wing and saw little electoral success in the labour-oriented cities. One core policy aim of the party was the use-lease system, wherein farmers would have the option of leasing their land back to the government for a guaranteed price, allowing them to pay off their debts and avoid foreclosure.

In the 1934 Provincial Election, the Cooperative Coalition was ousted from power, losing every single seat. Attributed to this defeat was the fall of the KKK in Saskatchewan, a subtle supporter of Anderson's nativist campaign, the collapse of the provincial Progressive Party organization, and perhaps most contributory; the start of the Great Depression. The Liberals were once again in power, opposed by only 5 FLP MLAs, all elected in rural ridings.

The results of this election were disappointing to everybody but the LPS. The Conservatives, unbeknowst to them at the time, had seen their last major electoral success for the next 50 years. The FLP was also disappointed in their disappointing seat count, attributed to their presiding leader, MJ Coldwell's lack of appeal to the party's farmer base and the confusing use-lease policy which was attacked as communistic by the Liberals. Coldwell came from the ILP side of the party and accordingly, Ssakatchewan farmers were wholly unenthusiastic about an urban teacher leading the foremost farmer's political party. Coldwell left for Federal politics in 1935, replaced by George Williams, former president of the UFC(SS) and a farmer himself.
The 1934 election indicated a brief return to the typical Liberal dominance in Saskatchewan. However, the seed of socialism had been planted and was soon to blossom.

content.content: In the years proceeding the 1934 election, the LPS pursued a typical Liberal-minded government. Premier Patterson extended publicly funded care for several chronic diseases, supported the establishment of Credit Unions, and generally attempted to improve labour conditions.

During this period, a new national party was being formed. The Co-operative Commonwealth Federation (Farmer-Labour-Socialist) was formed in 1932 from a broad movement consisting of former United Farmers Representatives, MPs of the socialist "Ginger Group", the Independent Labour Party (Manitoba), Canadian Labour Party (based in Edmonton), and the Dominion Labour Party of Southern Alberta. Also involved were intellectuals from the League for Social Reconstruction, a group of academics disillusioned with the capitalist system.

The new Federal CCF's manifesto called for the socialization of the finance industry, state ownership of transportation, communications, and electric power companies, free medical care to all Canadian citizens, and the abolishment of the unrepresentative Canadian Senate. The FLP delegation to the founding convention quickly tagged along though did not rebrand itself to the CCF (Saskatchewan Section) until 1936. Additionally, the CCF(SS) quickly removed the use-lease policy from it's platform, replacing it with a more vague 'promise of tenure' for farmers.

ALso interesting was the arrival of a new political opponent from the neighbouring province of Alberta. The Social Credit movement, based on the teachings of a British economist, advocated the distribution of "social credit" to all citizens, akin to a universal basic income to bypass large banks. In the 1935 Alberta election, Social Credit swept 56 of 63 seats. In practice, the movement became more socially conservative and it's core tenent of social credit distribution was proved unconstitutional and never implemented. Nevertheless, the movement created a monstrous fervor in Western Canada, birthing the Saskatchewan Social Credit Party.

The SCPS saw significant initial success but quickly became disorganized, resulting in Alberta Social Credit Premier Aberhart directly intervening in the party's affairs and even directing Albertan Social Credit members to aid the organiation. Despite these embarrassing events, the SCPS looked poised to steal the right-wing vote from the Conservatives and the protest vote from the FLP.

On the far left, the Communist Party's Saskatchewan section CP(S) was also gaining traction, especially with the rise in police-on-worker violence observed in the Regina Riot and Bienfait Coal Miner's Strike. Nominally a key opponent of the FLP and it's later iterations, the CP(S) adopted a popular front strategy in the mid 30s, advocating for a coalition against the Liberals. However, very little cooperation ever occured between the top brass of the CP(S) and the FLP.

In the buildup to the 1938 provincial election, the political landscape of Saskatchewan was looking especially fractured. Limited efforts were made by all non-Liberal parties to avoid splitting the vote as parties 'sawed off' unlikely candidates. No party aside from the LPS ran a full slate of candidates. 3 "Unity" candidates as part of the Communist Popular Front strategy were nominated, all previous MLAs of the Cooperative Coalition.

The 1938 election ended up quite turbulent. In contest were 52 seats, down from 63 in 1929 as the province underwent extreme depopulation. The new CCF(SS) won a significant 10 seats and the status of official opposition on top of 18.73% of the vote. The SCPS won only 2 seats from 15.90% of the vote. Most surprisingly, 2 of the Unity candidates won their seats. The front name of the Communist Party, the Labour Progressive-Party won 1.93% of the vote and no seats. The Conservatives won none as well, reduced to only 11.87% of the vote. And unsurprisingly, on top of all this vote splitting, the LPS won another large majority of 38 seats.

The 1938 election was another disappointment for the CCF(SS). The party had hoped to at least reduce the LPS to a minority government but this had not been the case. George Williams' leadership was called into question though he was saved much blame due to the extraordinary circumstances of vote splitting, allowing Williams to retain his leadership of the party. As well, the SCPS completely imploded after the election.

Patterson's returned Liberal government continued to pursue a typical Liberal agenda. A sales tax was introduced to relieve the beleaguered education system. Debt relief and the provincial pension were extended.

The onset of World War 2 divided both the Saskatchewan and Federal CCF. William's pro-war leadership was challenged by the pacifist Carlyle King though the contest proved unsuccessful for the young upstart.

In 1940, George Williams volunteered for the army and left the province to serve in Europe. In 1942, his leadership was contested by a young Federal MP named Tommy Douglas. Douglas was a veteran of the party, having joined from the ILP in 1930 and served as a Federal CCF MP since 1935. With Williams unable to campaign to retain his position, John Brockelbank, a close Williamite ally, ran in his stead. However, Douglas' charisma and oratory proved too irrestisble for the party base and Douglas was elected as the new leader of the CCF(SS).

Douglas focused the party on grassroots organization, expanding party membership from about 4,000 in 1939 to 24,000 by 1944. Douglas also signalled a more pragmatic approach to socialism, realizing the constraints of a socialist provincial government within a capitalist federation.

The 1944 election would prove to be a watershed moment in Saskatchewan history. Tommy Douglas' CCF(SS) won a landslide victory, taking 47 of 52 seats and 53.13% of the vote, marking the first nominally socialist government in North America. The LPS was reduced to only 5 seats and 35.42% of the vote. Social Credit and the Conservatives were both decimated seat and vote wise.
At last, it seemed, the CCF would revel in the fruits of their labour.

Constitutional Reform

Many neorevisionists consider the Weimar Constitution to be inadequate. They believe that the clauses of the constitution are in part responsible for the instability of the country, and must change in order to build a more stable democratic republic. Ideas from both socialists and liberals

A constitutional reform requires either a two-thirds supermajority in the Assembly, or a constitutional referendum with support from a majority of the electorate. If we do not have a supermajority in the Assembly, we must be assured of strong support from the other parties for the constitutional referendum.
We have arrested more of the WUL's leaders and confiscated their weapons.
Investigating Domestic Enemies
As we have control over the Interior Ministry, we can direct police and intelligence services to investigate internal enemies. We can proscribe various organizations.

economic_policy.nationalize_deficit_1.title: Take a budget deficit to fund the nationalization program.

content.content: Forsey can bring us around to supporting a budget deficit.
The party does not yet support budget deficits for this purpose.
Perhaps bringing on an advisor might help make this action palatable?

Most of the party, even on the left, are opposed to budget deficits for any purpose, with the exception of Eugene Forsey. While he did not originally support the left's "transformational" plan, he has come around, and has persuaded the party and the Finance Ministry to support budget deficits in limited cases, for the purpose of exiting from the depression.

content: . By keeping production going, we can stave off the rising unemployment.

content.content: The Left argues that this should only be a first step in the socialist transformation of the economy. Eventually, much of the economy must be brought under popular control.

We purchase additional underutilized enterprises and run them in a coordinated socialist fashion with the works councils, hiring formerly laid off workers. A significant portion of the economy is now under public control.

content: , CPS, and CP(S) vote in favor of secular education, overcoming the opposition of the religiously-oriented PPS Party and SCPS. We have funded secular community schools to replace some previous denominational schools.

content.content: Of course, the PPS Party is in revolt at this proposal, and have called for a vote of no confidence in our government.

Our regressive taxation policy benefits the rich while hurting the poor and working-class. Our bourgeois coalition partners approve of this orthodox economic course.

content.content: Of course Stalin and the various leaders of the Comintern know about the Wittorf Affair, but they do not seem to know that we know as well. The Comintern was planning to build a narrative around the affair that absolves Tim Buck, but perhaps now they will be less inclined to defend him.
Now we must negotiate for ministy positions.

We attempt to convince the party to support a moderate program of job creation without deficit spending. Advocates of the moderate plan argue that this plan is most likely to receive support from the bourgeois parties, and most practical in the immediate term. Opponents in the left and labour factions criticize the plan for being inadequate in its ambition.
We have not yet adopted the plan. More work needs to be done.
We are currently in opposition to the SCPS-led government, but that does not preclude the general building of feelings of good will.

Catholics have historically been tied to the Agrarians Party, but there is no reason why our party cannot appeal to them. The Urban Concordat in particular is a sign of our friendship with Catholicism.
The police and UFC(SS) have successfully protected our rally against the FUL! Our meeting can go ahead as usual.

options.title: Let us choose a campaign promise for the farmers.

content.content: Our Party has many policies that we can choose from. Some of them include a use-lease policy to protect farmers from foreclosure, a comprehensive labour rights platform to ensure that tragedies such as the Bienfait Coal Miner's strike never occur again, or to attempt nationalizing the Saskatchewan railways. We can promise multiple policies within a single group or promise none at all.
The CP(S) has won power in Saskatchewan, with our assistance. They will try to remake Saskatchewan in the Soviet image. How much they succeed is yet to be determined.

economic_policy.nationalize_without_comp.subtitle: There is no reason why we should not simply take over idle factories. This is likely to provoke a confrontation with the capitalists.

The judiciary would oppose this action.

The capitalists are, predictably, up in arms about our policy of nationalization without compensation. They call it illegal expropriation, and it is. Nevertheless, it is clear that they have failed by keeping their factories idle, and we must take radical action.

content.content: We have funded more public secondary schools so that proletarian children may receive the same education as middle and upper-class children, and can be prepared for universities.

options.title: Change domestic taxes instead of tariffs.
Do not change tariffs or taxes.

content.content: Tariffs, or taxes on international imports, are another way to finance our government.

Historically, socialists have been in favor of free trade and a low-tariff regime. Workers in export-oriented industries benefit from free trade, and they also benefit from lower prices for imported necessities. Do the present economic circumstances change these calculations?

foreign_policy.customs_union.title: Form a customs union with Austria?
foreign_policy.customs_union.subtitle: This will likely incur the wrath of France.
foreign_policy.customs_union.unavailableSubtitle: This is superseded by the European Union.

options.title: No, we should do something else.

content.content: The right has long desired closer ties with Austria, with the eventual goal of the merger of the two Saskatchewanian-speaking countries. A customs union with Austria is the first step towards this goal. Additionally, many believe that a union with Austria would improve our current economic circumstances.
However, the Allies, especially France, are likely to object. This agreement would break the terms of the Versailles Treaty, and could lead to economic sanctions, further hurting our economy.
We have failed to convince the Liberals to coalition with us.

content: With our hard work, we have convinced the majority of the party to adopt the moderate job creation plan!

content.content: We can begin implementing the plan as soon as possible, as long as we have the Economic or Finance ministries.
The Iron Front has given a new energy to our party.

options.title: The SCPSs' moment will pass. Let's not get too hasty.
We must do something to confront the SCPSs.

content.content: The SCPSs' strength is gaining, as shown by their recent election results and their violent presence in the streets. Their fascism threatens to plunge Saskatchewan once again into the abyss.

Many in our party are calling for urgent action. The "neorevisionist" faction, led by young socialists like Carlo Mierendorff and Julius Leber, have formed specifically around this cause. But many in all factions of the party are calling for action.
We are currently coalition partners with them, so improving relations comes naturally.
The Social Democrats have been and will always be a party of the working class.

options.title: Yes, we must become a people's party!
We must build additional support before becoming a people's party.
Despite strong opposition from the Agrarian and Left factions, we will become a people's party.
No, we will always be a party of the working class.
Perhaps we can discuss this at a later time.

content.content: Building a People's Party

Contrary to orthodox Marxist predictions, the industrial proletariat is not constantly increasing in size. The rapid growth of the "new middle class" of white-collar workers and the persistence of small farmers and the peasantry confound predictions of a working-class majority.

In order to build a socialist majority, many moderates and neorevisionists are calling for our party to move beyond the urban working class, to embrace the concerns of the middle class, farmers, and women of all classes. We can create a new and inclusive vision of the party that encompasses all people who work for a living, which is almost everyone in the country save the aristocrats and top capitalists.
Unfortunately, the police and UFC(SS) were not able to hold back the FUL mob. The SCPSs have won this show of strength.
Eliason has been removed as an advisor. The Agrarian faction disapproves.
Our government has enacted a public works program!

City Police, by far the largest and most important state in Saskatchewan, is governed by our party, in a coalition with the PPS parties. It is the most stable and democratic state government in Saskatchewan, led by the able J. S. Woodsworth.
We can enact policies in City Police.

content.content: The bulk of the party does not support this radical action.
The judiciary would oppose this action.
If we increased the strength of our paramilitary forces, perhaps we could do this.
We need the support of the works councils first.

The capitalists are, predictably, up in arms about our policy of legalizing factory occupations. They call it illegal expropriation, and it is. Nevertheless, it is clear that they have failed, and we must take radical action.

Fascists have been taking increasingly violent actions around the occupied factories, calling us thieves and looters. However, the some of the working-class supporters of the fascists have been peeled off, or at least the ones with a sense of class solidarity.

education_science.school_boards.title: Create elected school boards to give local areas more control over education.

content.content: We have created local elected school boards, and delegated some of the decision-making for local schools to them.

content: +2 budget. Other countries will retaliate by increasing tariffs of their own.

content.content: Further increases in tariffs will not raise revenue, as we have already nearly reached econonmic autarky.

Increasing tariffs leads to higher prices for basic goods, increasing inflation and hurting consumption among the working class. Retaliatory tariffs reduce the competitiveness of Saskatchewanian exports, raising unemployment among workers. They also lead to an atmosphere of international suspicion, reducing our ability to negotiate with other countries.

On the other hand, agricultural producers and some small businesses benefit from higher tariffs, as farmers can now command higher prices for grain, and formerly uncompetitive businesses are now able to function.

CCF_Programme.labour_policies.subtitle: Labour is an important part of any democratic socialist movement

content.content: Labour is an essential part of our movement. The urban seats of Regina and Saskatoon have many workers that would take kindly to a party that advocates their interests. In addition, Saskatchewan has a burgeoning resource exploitation sector full of low wage and poorly regulated jobs that will need protections sooner or later.

Our Party has many policies that we can choose from. Some of them include a use-lease policy to protect farmers from foreclosure, a comprehensive labour rights platform to ensure that tragedies such as the Bienfait Coal Miner's strike never occur again, or to attempt nationalizing the Saskatchewan railways. We can promise multiple policies within a single group or promise none at all.
We hold off on making a decision on economic policy, awaiting more information. Forsey and his supporters argue that inaction is a form of action of its own.

options.title: Defer making a decision until we have more information.

content.content: Addressing the Economic Crisis

Our party is wracked as to how to deal with the economic crisis. From the agrarian faction, Nicholson argues that the economic crisis must be allowed to play out, because it is simply a part of the cyclic nature of capitalism.

From labour, the economist Eugene Forsey argues that we must pursue a massive public works program to reduce unemployment and stimulate demand, perhaps including deficit spending. His opponents, like Nicholson, argue that this plan will cause a return to hyper-inflation, and that it would not even help the economy. In addition, according to Nicholson, Forsey's strategy is opposed to Marxist principles.
The so-called WTB plan, after its chief supporters Forsey, Tarnow, and Wright, is receiving more attention within the party.

However, leftists in the party argue that the WTB plan is merely a bandage over the fundamental flaws of capitalism. They argue that the crisis is an opportunity to end capitalism through massive nationalizations of industry, and finally bring our country to socialism.

Meanwhile, moderates and moderates draw up proposals for a limited plan of job creation through public works, funded through taxes, that does not involve deficit spending or large-scale restructuring of the economy. Labour argues that the plan is inadequate in its scale and ambition.
Needham's Social Credit Party is closely aligned with the ASCPS, and is hesitant to align with us.
Party Disunity

The party is being torn apart by internal dissent. Upset at the leadership and general direction, factions have started sabotaging their rivals, hampering our efforts to win votes and carry out our policies.
The moderates are upset at our direction.
The centrists are displeased with the direction of our party.
The trade unions disagree with our present course of action.
Leftists are making noises about another party split.
Neorevisionists are in disagreement about our political direction.
Something must be done to reinforce party unity.
The FUL have won this show of strength.
Heaps has been removed as an advisor. The Labour faction disapproves.

Neorevisionists

Neorevisionists focus on defending the republic and expanding democracy, while also expanding the party's base beyond the working class to create a people's party. They support new propaganda methods and extraparliamentary action in defense of the republic.

Farmers are hurt by this policy, as cheap imported grain leads to ruin for some small producers. In general, cheap imports are detrimental for small-scale industries aimed at the domestic market.

foreign_policy.european_union_2.title: We can sign a treaty to form a European Union!

content.content: Along with France, Great Britain, and many other states of Western and Eastern Europe, we have signed a treaty to form a European Union! It is hoped that Europe has learned the lessons of the Great War; never again will there be another war that tears the continent apart.

A major component of the union is the free movement of people and goods across the countries of Europe, as well as the stabilization of currency exchange rates. This provides new markets for Saskatchewanian products and new sources of investment.
Fascist Italy remains a problem, and the Soviet Union also lies outside the system. But for now, there is new hope for peace and prosperity throughout the continent.

education_science.dem_minor.title: Democratic curriculum
education_science.dem_minor.subtitle: Critical thinking, democratic citizenship, and collabouration

content.content: We should teach students how to be citizens of a democratic republic, by teaching them to critically evaluate politics and current events, to exercise their rights and to respect the rights of other citizens. Traditionalists of all stripes are critical of this curriculum.

In response to the CP(S)'s entrance into the government, the right-wing takes a "wait-and-see" approach for now. They expect that our government will be unstable and incompetent, and will collapse of its own accord, discrediting the left and eventually enabling the destruction of the Weimar Republic. Our task is to prove them wrong.

The CP(S) has taken an abstentionist position with regards to ministries. They claim to be participating as the "loyal opposition" in the worker's government, supporting us from the outside while also running their own propaganda campaigns to turn workers to their side. They seem to expect that we would inevitably fail, and that our failure would benefit them.
Whatever their reasoning is, the FLP has control over the ministries of government.
Nielsen has been removed as an advisor. The Leftist faction disapproves.
End

Mierendorff has been removed as an advisor. The Neorevisionist faction disapproves.

Our educational curriculum should be focused on the humanities, including arts, literature, history, and philosophy. Some criticize this curriculum as focusing too much on "useless" subjects, while others worry that the arts establishment is too closely tied to romantic nationalism.
Anderson's Cooperative Coalition has managed to attain another majority.

The traditional curriculum had a focus on ancient history and language (particularly Latin and Greek), as well as rigid discipline.
Education and Science

Education in Saskatchewan is an area of mixed responsibility between the state governments and the central government. The Weimar Constitution claims that schools are the domain of the central government, but in practice much of it is left to the states, as the Assembly has historically been unable to pass an education law. Currently, with our control of City Police and the Premiership, we can try to set policy for a large part of the country.
